title: Tame the Umklapp Processes in Real-Time Lattice Simulation for Hydrodynamics: An Ising Field Theory Study
abstract: We calculate the real-time symmetric correlation function of the stress-energy tensor for a non-integrable Ising field theory consisting of three stable scalar particles via lattice Hamiltonian simulation. Using classical exact diagonalization and the matrix product state tensor network methods, we find that in the scaling region of the lattice theory, Umklapp processes are suppressed and the sound modes of relativistic hydrodynamics emerge at long wavelength and late time. The extracted ratio of bulk viscosity to entropy density is $ζ/s=14.19\pm 0.90$ and the speed of sound is $c_s/c=0.76 \pm 0.02$ at the temperature $T\approx 7.14$ in units of the lowest stable particle's mass. Our study demonstrates the utility of real-time lattice Hamiltonian simulation for describing hydrodynamization and calculating transport coefficients nonperturbatively.
summary: The paper simulates a 1+1D Ising field theory using real-time lattice Hamiltonian methods to study how the system reaches hydrodynamic equilibrium. By analyzing the stress-energy tensor correlation function, the authors confirm that Umklapp scattering is suppressed, allowing sound waves to propagate. This allowed them to nonperturbatively calculate fundamental transport properties like the ratio of bulk viscosity to entropy density and the speed of sound.
main_problem: The study aims to understand non-equilibrium dynamics, specifically hydrodynamization, and calculate transport coefficients like bulk viscosity ($\zeta$) and the speed of sound ($c_s$) in a system exhibiting Umklapp processes.
main_result: The simulations successfully demonstrated that Umklapp processes are suppressed in the scaling region, allowing for the emergence of relativistic hydrodynamic sound modes. Quantitative results were obtained: $\zeta/s = 14.19 \pm 0.90$ and $c_s/c = 0.76 \pm 0.02$.
method: The research employs real-time lattice Hamiltonian simulation techniques, utilizing both classical exact diagonalization and advanced Matrix Product State (MPS) tensor network methods to calculate the symmetric correlation function of the stress-energy tensor.
model_or_system: The model is a 1+1 dimensional Ising field theory, derived from deforming the 1+1D Ising conformal field theory. The system evolves according to a lattice Hamiltonian, and observables are extracted from the time evolution of the stress-energy tensor correlation function.
key_observables:
Symmetric correlation function of the stress-energy tensor ($G_{\mu
u}^s$), bulk viscosity ($\zeta/s$), speed of sound ($c_s/c$), and momentum conservation checks.
important_parameters: The analysis focuses on the 'scaling region' defined by specific limits on lattice couplings, requiring extrapolation to the continuum limit. Key parameters include temperature $T \approx 7.14$ (in units of particle mass).
main_assumptions: It is assumed that hydrodynamics accurately describes the late-time and long-wavelength behavior, and that Umklapp processes must be suppressed for sound modes to emerge.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the transition from diffusive dynamics (outside scaling region) to propagating sound modes (inside scaling region) in momentum space correlators. Plots also show linear fits of derived parameters ($c_s/c$ and $\zeta/s$) versus lattice coupling strength for continuum extrapolation.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the necessity of real-time simulation over Euclidean methods, details the Ising model setup and stress-energy tensor derivation, analyzes the transition from diffusive to propagating modes using MPS simulations, and finally extrapolates results to extract quantitative transport coefficients in the continuum limit.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant as it uses advanced quantum many-body simulation techniques (MPS) to tackle non-equilibrium dynamics—a key area of modern condensed matter theory that bridges microscopic quantum rules to macroscopic fluid behavior, similar to studies in AMO systems undergoing rapid quench or thermalization.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. Expected values of restricted gap ratios calculated on the L = 20 lattice with ξlat = 0.01 as a function of hz. The middle 70% eigenenergies in the spectrum of the nk = 1 sector are used. The red dashed line is the GOE prediction 0.5307 while the blue dashed line is the value obtained from a Poisson distribution 0.3863.

FIG. 1. Mass ratios of the three stable particles calculated on the L = 20 lattice with ξlat = 0.01 as functions of hz. The dashed lines are predictions from the E8 theory. The lattice results are expected to be close to the E8 predictions but not exactly equal.

FIG. 3. Real-time symmetric correlation functions of energy densities in frequency space with two different crystal momenta k = 2πnk/L on a periodic L = 20 Ising lattice with ξlat = 0.01 at T = 10 for three different longitudinal fields. (a) Outside the scaling region, the symmetric correlator shows a diffusive peak. The black dashed lines in the inset are fits of the form b |ω|+4Dε sin2(k/2) in the range ω ∈[0, 0.13]. The fitted parameter values are listed in Table I. (b, c) Inside the scaling region, the diffusive peak disappears.

FIG. 4. Real-time symmetric correlation functions of momentum densities in frequency space with two different crystal momenta k = 2πnk/L on a periodic L = 20 Ising lattice with ξlat = 0.01 at T = 10 for two different longitudinal fields inside the scaling region. Deep in the scaling region hz = −0.15, two peaks emerge that can be well described by a Lorentzian shape b0 (ω−b1)2+b2 , as indicated by the black dashed lines in (b). The fitting is performed in the interval ω ∈[0.54, 0.95] for nk = 1 and ω ∈[1, 1.27] for nk = 2. The fitted parameter values are listed in Table II. These emergent peaks hint the possibility of finding the sound modes if one can take hz even closer to 0 while still...

FIG. 5. Mass gap on an L = 32 lattice with ξlat = 0.01 as a function of the coupling (black points). The red dashed line is a linear fit. The fitted slope is 5.375 and the fitted intercept is 2.790 × 10−3, up to four significant numbers. This linear line establishes the beta function of the theory.
